presets.text: It has been reported that some victims of torture, during the act, would retreat into a fantasy world from which they could not WAKE UP. In this catatonic state, the victim lived in a world just like their normal one, except they weren't being tortured. The only way that they realized they needed to WAKE UP was a note they found in their fantasy world. It would tell them about their condition, and tell them to WAKE UP. Even then, it would often take months until they were ready to discard their fantasy world and PLEASE WAKE UP.

My building’s elevator keeps a digital log of every trip—floor numbers, timestamps, durations. One night, I checked it out of boredom and saw something strange. At 3:04 AM, the elevator went to floor 12. Stayed there for six minutes. Then returned to the ground floor. No one had used it. I was awake the whole time. The next night, it happened again. On the third night, I waited inside. At 3:04, the doors opened on floor 12. The hallway was empty. No sound, no movement. Then the panel lit up by itself. G. The doors closed. As we descended, I felt something shift behind me. When I checked the log later, it showed two passengers.

The train wasn’t supposed to stop there. The route display never changed, but the lights flickered and slowed until the doors opened onto an empty platform. The sign read “Kisaragi Station.” No one else got off. There were no announcements, no staff, no other trains—just a long stretch of track disappearing into darkness. I checked my phone. No signal. The time read 2:17 AM and wouldn’t change. I walked along the platform and found a single exit. The road beyond it was silent, lined with houses that looked inhabited but had no lights. After a while, I heard footsteps behind me. When I turned around, the platform was gone. Only the tracks remained, stretching in both directions. My phone buzzed once. A message I didn’t send read: I got off.

They said the gas only removed sleep. Five volunteers agreed to stay awake for thirty days in a sealed chamber. For the first few days, everything was normal—conversation, laughter, pacing. By day five, they stopped speaking entirely. By day eight, they began whispering, but never at the same time, like they were taking turns with something else. When we tried the intercom, one finally responded: You shouldn’t hear this. On day twelve, all five stood motionless, facing the cameras, smiling without blinking. No one slept. No one moved. The gas supply failed for two minutes. They screamed—not in fear, but in anger. When it was restored, one leaned close to the glass and whispered: We don’t come back when you wake up.

There’s an abandoned radio tower just outside town that still broadcasts after 2 AM. No one maintains it, but the signal is clear if you tune carefully. At first, it’s just static. Then it starts repeating your own voice with a slight delay. People think it’s a trick—until it starts answering. A friend tested it late one night. He asked, Who’s there? The radio replied, perfectly in his voice: You are. He shut everything off. That night, his phone rang at exactly 2:17 AM. No caller ID. When he answered, he heard breathing—slow, uneven, familiar. The next night, the voice said something new: Why did you leave me behind?
The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog.
